content:
# Acts Chapter 5 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios from Quire 86 (PDF pages 230-231) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_005_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_005_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

### Ananias and Sapphira

1 But a certain man named Ananias, with Sapphira his wife, sold a piece of property,

2 and kept back part of the price, his wife also being aware of it, and brought a certain portion and laid it at the feet of the apostles.

3 But Peter said, "Ananias, why has Satan filled your heart to lie to the Holy Spirit and to keep back part of the price of the land?

4 While it remained unsold, did it not remain your own? And after it was sold, was it not at your disposal? How is it that you conceived this deed in your heart? You did not lie to men but to God."

5 And when Ananias heard these words, he fell down and breathed his last. And great fear came upon all who heard of it.

6 And the young men arose, wrapped him up, carried him out, and buried him.

7 Now it came to pass, after an interval of about three hours, that his wife came in, not knowing what had happened.

8 And Peter answered her, "Tell me whether you sold the land for this much?" And she said, "Yes, for that much."

9 And Peter said to her, "How is it that you agreed together to test the Spirit of the Lord? Behold, the feet of those who buried your husband are at the door, and they will carry you out."

10 And she fell down immediately at his feet and breathed her last. And the young men came in and found her dead, and they carried her out and buried her beside her husband.

11 And great fear came upon the whole church and upon all who heard of these things.

### Signs and Wonders of the Apostles

12 Now by the hands of the apostles many signs and wonders were being done among the people. And they were all with one accord in Solomon's Portico.

13 But none of the rest dared to join them, yet the people held them in high esteem.

14 And all the more, believers were being added to the Lord, multitudes of both men and women,

15 so that they even carried the sick out into the streets and laid them on cots and mats, that as Peter came by, at least his shadow might fall on some of them.

16 And the multitude from the cities surrounding Jerusalem also gathered, bringing the sick and those afflicted by unclean spirits, and they were all being healed.

### The Apostles Arrested and Delivered

17 But the high priest rose up, and all who were with him — the existing sect of the Sadducees — and they were filled with jealousy,

18 and they laid hands on the apostles and put them in the public prison.

19 But an angel of the Lord during the night opened the doors of the prison and led them out and said,

20 "Go, stand in the temple and speak to the people all the words of this Life."

21 And when they heard this, they entered the temple at daybreak and began to teach. Now when the high priest and those with him arrived, they called together the council, even the whole senate of the sons of Israel, and sent to the prison to have them brought.

22 But when the officers came, they did not find them in the prison, and they returned and reported,

23 saying, "We found the prison shut and locked with all security, and the guards standing at the doors, but when we opened them, we found no one inside."

24 Now when the captain of the temple and the chief priests heard these words, they were perplexed concerning them, wondering what this might become.

### The Apostles before the Council

25 Then someone came and reported to them, "Behold, the men whom you put in the prison are standing in the temple and teaching the people."

26 Then the captain went with the officers and brought them, but without violence, for they were afraid of the people, lest they be stoned.

27 And when they had brought them, they set them before the council. And the high priest questioned them,

28 saying, "We strictly commanded you not to teach in this name, and behold, you have filled Jerusalem with your teaching, and you intend to bring this man's blood upon us."

29 But Peter and the apostles answered and said, "We must obey God rather than men.

30 The God of our fathers raised up Jesus, whom you killed by hanging him on a tree.

31 God exalted him at his right hand as Leader and Savior, to give repentance to Israel and forgiveness of sins.

32 And we are witnesses of these things, and so is the Holy Spirit, whom God has given to those who obey him."

### Gamaliel's Counsel

33 And when they heard this, they were enraged and wanted to put them to death.

34 But a Pharisee in the council named Gamaliel, a teacher of the law held in honor by all the people, stood up and ordered the men to be put outside for a short time.

35 And he said to them, "Men of Israel, take care what you are about to do with these men.

36 For before these days Theudas rose up, claiming to be somebody, and a number of men, about four hundred, joined him. He was put to death, and all who followed him were dispersed and came to nothing.

37 After him, Judas the Galilean rose up in the days of the census and drew away people after him. He also perished, and all who followed him were scattered.

38 And now I say to you, keep away from these men and let them alone, for if this plan or this work is of men, it will be overthrown;

39 but if it is of God, you will not be able to overthrow them — lest you even be found fighting against God." And they were persuaded by him.

40 And they called in the apostles and beat them and charged them not to speak in the name of Jesus, and released them.

41 So they went out from the presence of the council, rejoicing that they were counted worthy to suffer dishonor for the sake of the Name.

42 And every day, in the temple and from house to house, they did not cease teaching and proclaiming the good news that the Christ is Jesus.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

**General agreement:** The three translators show strong alignment across Acts 5. The Ananias and Sapphira narrative (vv. 1-11), the summary of apostolic signs (vv. 12-16), the second arrest and angelic deliverance (vv. 17-26), the hearing before the Sanhedrin (vv. 27-32), and Gamaliel's counsel (vv. 33-42) are rendered consistently in substance across all three agents. Most differences concern register, interpretive expansion, and the degree to which Greek morphology is carried into English. EMET preserves Greek participial chains and word order; LOGOS provides formal English equivalence with measured clarity; PNEUMA aims for contemporary accessibility and dramatic impact.

**Manuscript observations:** The first Sinaiticus image (PDF page 230) covers Acts 5:3 through approximately 5:23, spread across four columns. The second image (PDF page 231) covers approximately Acts 5:30 through Acts 6:7, also in four columns. The uncial text is legible and consistent with the standard Sinaiticus transcription. At verse 9, the Sinaiticus reading confirms συνεφωνήθη ("was agreed upon"), supporting the passive construction "it was agreed upon by you" rather than an active "you agreed." At verse 17, the text reads ἡ οὖσα αἵρεσις τῶν Σαδδουκαίων ("the existing sect of the Sadducees"), a reading preserved in EMET and adopted in the consensus. At verse 28, the Sinaiticus reading παραγγελίᾳ παρηγγείλαμεν ("with a command we commanded") confirms the cognate dative construction that all three translators recognize. At verse 32, the Sinaiticus reads ῥημάτων ("words/things"), which EMET renders literally as "words" while LOGOS and PNEUMA render as "things." The consensus follows LOGOS with "things," which captures the broader semantic range of ῥῆμα in this context. No significant scribal corrections or textual variants are visible in the Acts 5 portion that would affect translation decisions.

**Key decisions:**

1. **Verse 1 — "Now" vs. "But" (δέ):** EMET: "Now a certain man." LOGOS: "But a certain man." PNEUMA: "But a man." The Greek δέ is a mild connective that can be transitional ("now, and") or mildly adversative ("but"). In context, Acts 5:1 contrasts Ananias with Barnabas (4:36-37), whose generosity was genuine. The adversative "But" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) better captures this contrast: Barnabas gave freely, but Ananias deceived. I follow LOGOS.

2. **Verse 2 — "kept back" (ἐνοσφίσατο):** All three translators render ἐνοσφίσατο similarly — EMET: "kept-back," LOGOS: "kept back part of the price," PNEUMA: "kept back part of the money." The Greek ἐνοσφίσατο means "to set apart for oneself, to embezzle, to misappropriate." It is the same verb used in the LXX of Joshua 7:1 for Achan's theft from the devoted things — an allusion Luke almost certainly intends. "Kept back" captures the essential meaning. PNEUMA's substitution of "money" for "price" (τιμῆς) is acceptable but less precise. I follow LOGOS with "kept back part of the price."

3. **Verse 4 — "conceived" vs. "placed" vs. "think up" (ἔθου ἐν τῇ καρδίᾳ σου):** EMET: "you-placed in the heart of-you this deed." LOGOS: "you have conceived this deed in your heart." PNEUMA: "What made you think up this scheme?" The Greek ἔθου ἐν τῇ καρδίᾳ literally means "you placed in the heart" — a Semitic idiom meaning to resolve or determine inwardly. LOGOS's "conceived" captures the idiom naturally. PNEUMA restructures as a rhetorical question ("What made you think up this scheme?"), which departs from the Greek syntax. EMET's literal "placed in the heart" is wooden. I follow LOGOS with "conceived this deed in your heart." I also follow LOGOS in rendering the opening rhetorical questions as statements with question marks rather than EMET's inverted participial structure.

4. **Verse 5 — "breathed his last" vs. "expired" vs. "died" (ἐξέψυξεν):** EMET: "expired." LOGOS: "breathed his last." PNEUMA: "collapsed and died." The Greek ἐξέψυξεν (from ἐκψύχω) means literally "breathed out" — the life-breath departing. This is a distinctively Lukan word, used only in Acts (5:5, 5:10, 12:23). LOGOS's "breathed his last" best captures the etymology (ψυχή = breath/soul + ἐκ = out). PNEUMA adds "collapsed" to intensify the drama — the Greek has only πεσών ("having fallen") and ἐξέψυξεν ("expired"), so "collapsed and died" conflates and paraphrases. EMET's "expired" is accurate but clinical. I follow LOGOS.

5. **Verse 8 — "answered her" vs. "responded to her" vs. "confronted her" (ἀπεκρίθη):** EMET: "answered to her." LOGOS: "responded to her." PNEUMA: "confronted her." The Greek ἀπεκρίθη is a standard word for "answered, responded." PNEUMA's "confronted" adds adversarial force not present in the verb itself — while the context is indeed confrontational, the Greek uses the neutral ἀποκρίνομαι. I follow LOGOS with "answered her," simplifying "responded" to the more natural "answered."

6. **Verse 9 — "agreed together" vs. "it was agreed upon by you" (συνεφωνήθη ὑμῖν):** EMET: "it-was-agreed-upon by-you." LOGOS: "you have agreed together." PNEUMA: "the two of you agree." The Sinaiticus reading confirms the passive form συνεφωνήθη ("it was agreed upon"). LOGOS and PNEUMA convert to the active voice for natural English. EMET preserves the Greek passive. I follow LOGOS with the active rendering "you agreed together," which is idiomatic while preserving the sense of joint complicity that the συν- prefix conveys.

7. **Verse 11 — "church" (ἐκκλησίαν):** EMET: "assembly." LOGOS: "church." PNEUMA: "church." The Greek ἐκκλησία means "assembly, gathering, called-out ones." This is the first use of ἐκκλησία in Acts for the Christian community (previously it appeared in the LXX sense of "assembly of Israel"). EMET's "assembly" preserves the secular/LXX meaning of the word. LOGOS and PNEUMA use "church," which is the standard rendering for the Christian community. Since Luke is now using the term for the specifically Christian body of believers, "church" is appropriate. I follow LOGOS and PNEUMA.

8. **Verse 12 — "Solomon's Portico" vs. "Porch of Solomon" vs. "Solomon's Colonnade":** EMET: "Porch of-Solomon." LOGOS: "Solomon's Portico." PNEUMA: "Solomon's Colonnade." The Greek στοᾷ τοῦ Σολομῶνος refers to the covered walkway on the eastern side of the temple. "Portico" (LOGOS) is the standard architectural term for a roofed colonnade. "Porch" (EMET) is too small-scale; "Colonnade" (PNEUMA) is architecturally specific but less traditional. I follow LOGOS with "Solomon's Portico," consistent with Acts 3:11 in our prior consensus.

9. **Verse 15 — "cots and mats" vs. "small-beds and mats" (κλιναρίων καὶ κραβάττων):** EMET: "small-beds and mats." LOGOS: "cots and mats." PNEUMA: "cots and mats." The Greek κλινάριον is a diminutive of κλίνη ("bed"), hence "small bed, cot." The Greek κράβαττος is a loanword (Latin grabatus) meaning a pallet or mat. LOGOS and PNEUMA agree on "cots and mats," which is clear and accurate. I follow the consensus.

10. **Verse 17 — "the existing sect of the Sadducees" (ἡ οὖσα αἵρεσις τῶν Σαδδουκαίων):** EMET: "the existing sect of-the Sadducees." LOGOS: "that is, the sect of the Sadducees." PNEUMA: "the party of the Sadducees." The Greek participle οὖσα ("being, existing") modifies αἵρεσις. This phrase identifies the high priest's allies as belonging to the Sadducean party. EMET preserves the participle. LOGOS renders it as an explanatory parenthetical. PNEUMA drops it entirely. The Sinaiticus reading confirms ἡ οὖσα αἵρεσις. I render "the existing sect of the Sadducees" with EMET's literal approach, since the participle may carry a subtle Lukan nuance — identifying the party that "was" or "existed" at that time — which is lost if reduced to a simple gloss.

11. **Verse 20 — "this Life" (τῆς ζωῆς ταύτης):** EMET: "this life." LOGOS: "this Life" (capitalized). PNEUMA: "this Life" (capitalized). The Greek τῆς ζωῆς ταύτης could mean "this life" (ordinary existence) or, more theologically, "this Life" — the message of eternal life in Christ. The context is an angelic command to proclaim the gospel, so the theological sense predominates. LOGOS and PNEUMA capitalize to signal this. I follow the consensus with "this Life."

12. **Verse 21 — "senate" vs. "council-of-elders" vs. "full council of Israel's elders" (γερουσίαν):** EMET: "council-of-elders." LOGOS: "senate." PNEUMA: "full council of Israel's elders." The Greek γερουσία means "council of elders, senate" — from γέρων ("elder"). This term appears only here in the NT and refers to the broader assembly of Israelite elders, possibly a body overlapping with but distinct from the Sanhedrin proper (συνέδριον). LOGOS's "senate" is a precise Greco-Roman equivalent. EMET's "council-of-elders" is etymologically transparent. PNEUMA expands with "full council of Israel's elders." I follow LOGOS with "senate," which preserves the technical terminology and its distinction from "council" (συνέδριον) used in the same verse. The qualifier "of the sons of Israel" makes the referent clear.

13. **Verse 24 — "perplexed" vs. "greatly perplexed" vs. "baffled" (διηπόρουν):** EMET: "were-perplexed." LOGOS: "were greatly perplexed." PNEUMA: "were baffled." The Greek διαπορέω means "to be thoroughly at a loss, to be perplexed." The δια- prefix intensifies the root πορέω ("to be at a loss"). LOGOS captures this with "greatly perplexed." PNEUMA's "baffled" is strong but colloquial. EMET's "perplexed" loses the intensifying prefix. I follow the consensus with "were perplexed," acknowledging that the simple English "perplexed" already carries sufficient force, while adding "concerning them" from EMET to preserve the Greek construction.

14. **Verse 28 — "We strictly commanded" (παραγγελίᾳ παρηγγείλαμεν):** EMET: "With-a-command we-commanded." LOGOS: "We strictly commanded." PNEUMA: "We gave you strict orders." The Greek uses the cognate dative παραγγελίᾳ παρηγγείλαμεν — a Semitic intensifying construction ("with a command we commanded"). LOGOS renders this idiom as "strictly commanded," which captures the intensification. PNEUMA paraphrases similarly. EMET preserves the cognate structure. I follow LOGOS — the cognate dative is a Hebrew/Aramaic idiom (cf. Gen 2:17 LXX θανάτῳ ἀποθανεῖσθε) that "strictly" adequately conveys in English.

15. **Verse 29 — "men" vs. "human authorities" (ἀνθρώποις):** EMET: "men." LOGOS: "men." PNEUMA: "human authorities." The Greek ἀνθρώποις is simply "men, human beings." PNEUMA adds "authorities" as an interpretive gloss — contextually accurate (they are addressing the Sanhedrin), but it narrows the Greek's broader scope. Peter's declaration is a universal principle: God's authority supersedes all human authority, not just that of this particular council. I follow EMET and LOGOS with "men."

16. **Verse 30 — "killed" vs. "laid hands on" vs. "had killed" (διεχειρίσασθε):** EMET: "laid-hands-on." LOGOS: "killed." PNEUMA: "had killed." The Greek διαχειρίζομαι means literally "to take in hand, to lay hands on" and by extension "to slay, to kill." In Acts 26:21, Luke uses it unambiguously for attempted killing. Here the context makes clear that Peter accuses them of putting Jesus to death. EMET's "laid hands on" preserves the literal sense but is ambiguous in English (it could mean arrest). LOGOS and PNEUMA make the meaning explicit with "killed." I follow LOGOS.

17. **Verse 30 — "tree" vs. "cross" (ξύλου):** EMET: "tree." LOGOS: "tree." PNEUMA: "cross." The Greek ξύλον means "wood, tree, anything made of wood." When used of the crucifixion (Acts 5:30; 10:39; 13:29; Gal 3:13; 1 Pet 2:24), it deliberately echoes Deuteronomy 21:22-23 LXX: "cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree." This is not a neutral synonym for "cross" (σταυρός) — it is a theologically loaded term invoking the curse of the Law. PNEUMA's "cross" loses this crucial allusion. EMET and LOGOS correctly preserve "tree." I follow the EMET/LOGOS consensus.

18. **Verse 31 — "Leader" vs. "Prince" (ἀρχηγόν):** All three translators render ἀρχηγόν as "Leader" (EMET, LOGOS) or "Leader" (PNEUMA). This term (also used in Acts 3:15) means "originator, founder, leader, pioneer." The consensus "Leader" is appropriate and consistent with our Acts 3 rendering.

19. **Verse 32 — "these things" vs. "these words" (τῶν ῥημάτων τούτων):** EMET: "these words." LOGOS: "these things." PNEUMA: "these things." The Greek ῥῆμα means both "word" and "thing, matter, event." In this context, the apostles are witnessing not to spoken words but to the events of Jesus's death, resurrection, and exaltation. LOGOS and PNEUMA correctly render ῥημάτων as "things" in this sense. The Sinaiticus reading confirms ῥημάτων. I follow LOGOS.

20. **Verse 33 — "enraged" vs. "cut to the heart" (διεπρίοντο):** EMET: "were-cut-to-the-heart." LOGOS: "were enraged." PNEUMA: "were enraged." The Greek διαπρίω means literally "to saw through" — metaphorically, "to be cut to the heart, to be infuriated." This is a strong term denoting visceral fury (distinct from the piercing conviction of Acts 2:37, which uses κατανύσσομαι). "Enraged" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) captures the hostile outcome. EMET's "cut to the heart" could be confused with the conviction of Acts 2:37. I follow the LOGOS/PNEUMA consensus with "enraged."

21. **Verse 34 — word order and structure:** EMET preserves Greek participial order: "having-stood-up, a certain [one] in the council, a Pharisee, Gamaliel by-name." LOGOS and PNEUMA front "Gamaliel" into the description more naturally. I follow LOGOS's structure: "But a Pharisee in the council named Gamaliel, a teacher of the law held in honor by all the people, stood up and ordered the men to be put outside for a short time."

22. **Verse 36 — "claiming to be somebody" vs. "saying himself to be someone" (λέγων εἶναί τινα ἑαυτόν):** EMET: "saying himself to-be someone." LOGOS: "claiming to be somebody." PNEUMA: "claiming to be somebody important." The Greek τινα means simply "someone, somebody" — PNEUMA's addition of "important" is interpretive. LOGOS captures the self-aggrandizing claim without addition. I follow LOGOS.

23. **Verse 38 — "plan" vs. "counsel" vs. "plan" (βουλή):** EMET: "counsel." LOGOS: "plan." PNEUMA: "plan." The Greek βουλή means "purpose, plan, counsel, deliberate intention." In this context, Gamaliel is evaluating whether the Christian movement has divine backing. "Plan" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) works for the purposive sense. "Counsel" (EMET) has an archaic ring but preserves the deliberative nuance. I follow LOGOS with "plan."

24. **Verse 39 — "fighting against God" (θεομάχοι):** EMET: "God-fighters." LOGOS: "fighting against God." PNEUMA: "fighting against God." The Greek θεομάχοι is a compound adjective meaning "fighters against God." EMET's literal "God-fighters" is striking but non-standard English. LOGOS and PNEUMA expand it naturally. I follow the consensus with "fighting against God."

25. **Verse 41 — "suffer dishonor" vs. "to be dishonored" (ἀτιμασθῆναι):** EMET: "to-be-dishonored." LOGOS: "to suffer dishonor." PNEUMA: "to be dishonored." The Greek ἀτιμασθῆναι is the passive infinitive of ἀτιμάζω ("to dishonor, treat shamefully"). LOGOS's "suffer dishonor" adds the nuance of endurance. PNEUMA's "to be dishonored" is more direct. Both are valid. I follow LOGOS with "suffer dishonor," which better conveys the paradox of joyful suffering.

26. **Verse 42 — "the Christ is Jesus" vs. "the Messiah is Jesus" (τὸν χριστόν, Ἰησοῦν):** EMET: "the Christ, Jesus." LOGOS: "the Christ is Jesus." PNEUMA: "the Messiah is Jesus." The Greek τὸν χριστόν Ἰησοῦν can be read as an appositional construction ("the Christ, [that is,] Jesus") or a predicate construction ("that the Christ is Jesus"). The latter reading — proclaiming that the Messiah is identified as Jesus — is supported by the word order and by Luke's usage. PNEUMA's "Messiah" is the Hebrew equivalent of χριστός; "Christ" preserves the Greek. I follow LOGOS with "the Christ is Jesus," which maintains the Greek term and the predicate identification.

---
## Disagreement Log

### Verse 4 — ἔθου ἐν τῇ καρδίᾳ: "placed in the heart," "conceived," or "think up"?

**The variant:** The Semitic idiom for inward resolution is rendered with varying degrees of literalness.

- **EMET** rendered "you-placed in the heart of-you this deed."
- **LOGOS** rendered "you have conceived this deed in your heart."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "What made you think up this scheme?"

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS with "conceived this deed in your heart." The Greek ἔθου ἐν τῇ καρδίᾳ ("you placed in the heart") is a Semitic idiom meaning to determine or resolve inwardly. "Conceived" captures the idiom naturally in English. PNEUMA's "think up this scheme" restructures the sentence and adds "scheme" (not in the Greek), importing a connotation of deliberate plotting beyond what τὸ πρᾶγμα τοῦτο ("this deed/matter") conveys. EMET's literal "placed in the heart" is wooden but preserves the idiom's Semitic flavor.

### Verse 5 — ἐξέψυξεν: "breathed his last," "expired," or "died"?

**The variant:** The distinctively Lukan word for death is rendered with varying registers.

- **EMET** rendered "expired."
- **LOGOS** rendered "breathed his last."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "collapsed and died."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS with "breathed his last." The Greek ἐξέψυξεν (ἐκ + ψυχή) literally means "breathed out the soul/life-breath." This word occurs only in Acts (5:5, 5:10, 12:23) and is a deliberate Lukan choice. "Breathed his last" preserves the etymological connection to breath/spirit. PNEUMA's "collapsed and died" adds "collapsed" and reduces the specific verb to generic "died," losing the Lukan coloring. EMET's "expired" is etymologically parallel (Latin ex-spirare) but reads as clinical in modern English.

### Verse 8 — Peter's question: structure and additions

**The variant:** Peter's confrontation of Sapphira is rendered with different degrees of expansion.

- **EMET** rendered "Tell me if for-so-much you-gave-away the land?"
- **LOGOS** rendered "Tell me whether you sold the land for this much?"
- **PNEUMA** rendered "Tell me — is this the full price you and your husband received for the land?"

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS. The Greek εἰ τοσούτου τὸ χωρίον ἀπέδοσθε ("if for so much you sold the land") is a straightforward question. PNEUMA adds "the full price" and "you and your husband" — neither phrase is in the Greek. These additions make the deception more explicit but depart from the text's understated irony. Peter asks a simple question whose devastating implications emerge only with Sapphira's answer.

### Verse 11 — ἐκκλησίαν: "church" or "assembly"?

**The variant:** The first occurrence of ἐκκλησία for the Christian community in Acts.

- **EMET** rendered "assembly."
- **LOGOS** rendered "church."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "church."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS and PNEUMA with "church." The Greek ἐκκλησία means "assembly, gathering." EMET's "assembly" preserves the secular/LXX sense. However, this is the first time Luke applies ἐκκλησία to the Christian community in Acts, marking a significant terminological development. "Church" is the standard English rendering for this emerging institution, and its use here signals Luke's recognition that the believers constitute a distinct community. Rendering it as "assembly" obscures this ecclesiological milestone.

### Verse 17 — ἡ οὖσα αἵρεσις: "the existing sect" or "that is, the sect"?

**The variant:** The participial phrase identifying the Sadducean party is rendered differently.

- **EMET** rendered "the existing sect of-the Sadducees."
- **LOGOS** rendered "that is, the sect of the Sadducees."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "the party of the Sadducees."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows EMET with "the existing sect of the Sadducees." The Greek participle οὖσα ("being, existing") may carry a subtle Lukan nuance — perhaps identifying the party as the one that "existed" or held power at that time. LOGOS reduces this to a parenthetical gloss; PNEUMA drops the participle entirely. Preserving "existing" retains the Greek structure and its possible historiographic coloring.

### Verse 29 — ἀνθρώποις: "men" or "human authorities"?

**The variant:** The scope of the contrast with God's authority differs.

- **EMET** rendered "men."
- **LOGOS** rendered "men."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "human authorities."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows EMET and LOGOS with "men." The Greek ἀνθρώποις is simply "men, human beings" — it does not specify "authorities" (ἐξουσίαις). PNEUMA's addition narrows the universal principle to this specific confrontation. Peter's declaration — "We must obey God rather than men" — is a statement of absolute priority that transcends the council chamber. Adding "authorities" imports a gloss that limits the maxim's scope.

### Verse 30 — ξύλον: "tree" or "cross"?

**The variant:** The term used for the instrument of Jesus's death carries different allusive weight.

- **EMET** rendered "tree."
- **LOGOS** rendered "tree."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "cross."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows EMET and LOGOS with "tree." The Greek ξύλον means "wood, tree." When used of the crucifixion, it deliberately echoes Deuteronomy 21:22-23 (LXX): "cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree." This is not a neutral synonym for σταυρός ("cross") — it invokes the curse of the Torah, a theologically loaded allusion central to early Christian preaching (cf. Gal 3:13; 1 Pet 2:24). PNEUMA's "cross" provides clarity about the historical method of execution but sacrifices the Deuteronomic allusion that gives ξύλον its theological force in apostolic proclamation.

### Verse 32 — ῥημάτων: "words" or "things"?

**The variant:** The semantic range of ῥῆμα is captured differently.

- **EMET** rendered "these words."
- **LOGOS** rendered "these things."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "these things."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS and PNEUMA with "these things." The Greek ῥῆμα means both "word, utterance" and "thing, matter, event." In this context, the apostles witness not to spoken words but to the events they have described — Jesus's death, resurrection, and exaltation. The Sinaiticus reading confirms ῥημάτων. Rendering as "things" captures the broader semantic range appropriate to the context. EMET's "words" is lexically valid but narrows the referent.

### Verse 33 — διεπρίοντο: "cut to the heart" or "enraged"?

**The variant:** The metaphor of being "sawn through" is rendered with different emotional registers.

- **EMET** rendered "were-cut-to-the-heart."
- **LOGOS** rendered "were enraged."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "were enraged."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS and PNEUMA with "were enraged." The Greek διαπρίω means "to saw through" — metaphorically, to be deeply agitated or furious. This is distinct from Acts 2:37 (κατενύγησαν τὰς καρδίας, "they were pierced in heart"), where the response is conviction and repentance. Here the response is murderous rage (v. 33b: "wanted to put them to death"). EMET's "cut to the heart," while etymologically reflective of the metaphor, risks confusion with the very different reaction in Acts 2:37. "Enraged" makes the hostile outcome clear.

### Verse 36 — PNEUMA's addition of "important" to τινα

**The variant:** PNEUMA adds an interpretive word not in the Greek.

- **EMET** rendered "saying himself to-be someone."
- **LOGOS** rendered "claiming to be somebody."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "claiming to be somebody important."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS without the addition. The Greek τινα ("someone, somebody") is deliberately vague — Theudas claimed significance without the text specifying what kind. PNEUMA's "important" is a reasonable inference but an addition to the text. The vagueness of "somebody" mirrors the Greek and may even be subtly dismissive — Theudas claimed to be "somebody" but turned out to be nobody.

### Verse 42 — χριστόν: "Christ" or "Messiah"?

**The variant:** The Greek title is transliterated or translated.

- **EMET** rendered "the Christ, Jesus."
- **LOGOS** rendered "the Christ is Jesus."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "the Messiah is Jesus."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS with "the Christ is Jesus." The Greek χριστός is a translation of the Hebrew mashiach ("anointed one"). In Greek-language texts, χριστός functions as both a title and (increasingly) a proper name. PNEUMA's "Messiah" translates back into Hebrew, which is one step removed from the Greek source text. "Christ" preserves the Greek while retaining its titular force when preceded by the article (τὸν χριστόν). The predicate construction "the Christ is Jesus" reflects the apostolic proclamation that Jesus is the one who fulfills the messianic hope.
